订阅轰炸以及如何缓解

Subscription bombing and how to mitigate it

bytemash.net 作者 homelessdino 分数 282 评论 176 2026/04/02 12:08
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 几周前，我们注意到 Suga 身上有些奇怪的地方。新用户注册了，但没有做任何事情，他们没有创建组织、项目或部署，他们只是留下了一个帐户。大多数新用户与产品的交互速度非常快，我们会报告活动统计数据以尝试了解阻止程序，因此即使完全不活动的帐户中的小幅增长也会引人注目。然后我们查看了新用户的名称，它们是 PfVQXvYTXjwSbEeJBjXYy 和 xXzMafkbPLjOaGgDaOGZjLx 等条目。我们检查了重新发送（我们的电子邮件服务），可以看到欢迎电子邮件发出并发送到这些帐户。它们是真实的电子邮件地址，但名字却是垃圾……有些不对劲。
  2. 什么是订阅轰炸？订阅轰炸是一种攻击，其中有人使用机器人在数百或数千个网站上注册受害者的电子邮件地址。其目标不是访问这些网站上的帐户，而是让受害者的收件箱充满大量噪音，以致他们无法找到真正重要的电子邮件。而受害者却沉浸在“欢迎使用 SaaS 产品！”之中。和“验证您从未要求的新闻通讯的电子邮件”，攻击者正在做其他事情。他们重置受害者的银行密码，用他们的账户进行购物，或者以他们的名义注册信用卡。真正的安全警报和确认电子邮件被噪音淹没了。进行这些攻击的人正在窃取金钱并冒充真实的人或企业。互联网上每一个允许您输入任何电子邮件地址而无需验证的注册表单都是他们可以利用的工具。我们是如何发现的 3 月 12 日，有一个不寻常的注册开始，然后在接下来的两天里，我们每天看到另外 2-3 个注册，这个注册数量低得足以引起噪音。老实说，我们的第一个想法是有人在测试我们的服务。这很常见，我们在其他产品中也经历过这种情况，而且当涉及到负责任的披露时，我们很欣赏这种情况，所以它没有敲响任何警钟。到 3 月 14 日，我们有 6 个符合该模式的注册用户，并且我们在 PostHog 中注意到了其他情况。忘记密码页面的页面浏览量和互动量异常高。活动的结合足以让我们仔细观察。该模式每个机器人都会使用真实受害者的电子邮件地址和垃圾名称进行注册。然后，在大约 60 秒内，它将导航到忘记密码页面并请求重置。这意味着每个受害者在一分钟内收到我们发来的三封电子邮件：
  3. 验证您的电子邮件地址 欢迎来到 Suga 重置您的密码
  4. 他们从未要求过的三封电子邮件，来自他们可能从未听说过的产品。我们只是可能同时受到攻击的数百个网站之一。这些机器人还使用随机电子邮件地址访问忘记密码页面，大概是试图为已经是用户的受害者触发重置电子邮件。我们详细回顾了一次会话，打字行为很有趣。机器人在表单字段中输入值的速度极其缓慢，每次输入一个字符，两次按键之间的间隔长达一秒。这些间隙对他们来说有随机性，但也太随机了。人类会连续输入，大多数人快速输入几个字符，暂停，然后再次输入。这是一个平坦的延迟分布，试图看起来很人性化，但失败了。页面导航之间的时间安排具有相同的随机性，但统一如此。足够的变化来躲避简单的机器人检测，但不足以真正冒充真人。设计为隐形的 这就是订阅轰炸与大多数人想象的攻击如此不同的原因。没有出现峰值或请求泛滥，也没有出现剧烈的服务器负载。每小时只有 1-2 个注册，仅此而已。这些请求来自世界各地（印度、巴西、罗马尼亚、美国、越南、土耳其），除非将其与典型流量进行比较，否则这并不罕见。我们的真实用户通常从与该国家/地区白天时间有合理相关性的特定国家/地区进行导航。机器人流量在国家/地区和时间之间的相关性为零，这种不匹配是最突出的。速率限制在这里不起作用，因为您无法真正对每小时一个请求进行速率限制。这种攻击的重点是保持在阈值以下，这就是我发现这种攻击类型如此有趣的原因之一。损害不是针对您的订阅轰炸几乎不会伤害网站所有者，我们的电子邮件发件人声誉没有受到影响（数量太低）。如果我们没有密切跟踪用户激活情况，我们可能不会注意到。不幸的是，损失几乎全部是另一端的人。想象一下，一觉醒来有 200 多封来自您从未听说过的服务的电子邮件，您开始删除它们，但它们不断出现。在这堆垃圾中的某个地方有一条重要的通知，比如有人更改了您的银行电子邮件地址、重置了您的密码或以您的名义订购了一张新信用卡。这种攻击之所以有效，是因为成千上万的网站（新闻通讯、SaaS 产品、论坛、电子商务商店）允许任何人输入任何电子邮件地址并立即开始向其发送电子邮件。如果您的注册表单向未经验证的地址发送电子邮件，则您的表单属于其中的一部分。而且由于损害落在受害者身上，而不是网站所有者身上，我怀疑大多数人都将其视为低优先级修复，这是错误的。它会污染您的用户数据，并使您的服务成为骚扰真实用户的帮凶。我们做了什么 防火墙规则 我们的第一步是加强前端托管提供商防火墙上的机器人检测。这将体积大约减少了一半，但并没有消除。配置也很棘手，因为我们有合法的非浏览器流量访问我们需要继续工作的 API （网络钩子、服务到服务调用）。我们让它运行大约 6 小时。在那段时间里，又有两次报名失败（一个在 5 小时后，另一个在 6 小时后）。机器人已经通过了验证步骤，因此我们需要更好的东西。 Cloudflare Turnstile Cloudflare Turnstile 是解决方案，它是一种验证码替代方案，不会要求用户解决难题。它在后台分析浏览器信号，并且可以配置为仅在某些内容看起来不正常时提出可见的挑战。您可以对其进行设置，以便对于真实用户来说它是不可见的。我们使用 Better Auth 进行身份验证，它有一个内置的验证码插件，支持 Turnstile。这使得实现非常简单，只需在服务器端添加插件，将 Turnstile 组件拖放到身份验证页面上，例如使用 @marsidev/react-turnstile 进行注册、登录和忘记密码，然后通过标头将令牌传递到服务器。在 Turnstile 提供有效令牌之前，提交按钮将保持禁用状态。部署 Turnstile 后，机器人注册停止，因此我想在此特别提及 Better Auth 和 Cloudflare Turnstile。这种组合使得这个问题可以在当天修复。如果您的身份验证解决方案不支持开箱即用的验证码，Turnstile 的 API 仍然可以轻松直接集成，但将其内置可以节省我们的时间。仅向经过验证的用户发送电子邮件即使已安装 Turnstile，我们也希望在这种情况再次发生时限制损失。我们更新了电子邮件服务代码，以便用户在单击链接并证明他们拥有该地址之前，只会收到我们发送的一封电子邮件（验证电子邮件）。在验证之前没有欢迎电子邮件，没有产品更新，没有其他任何内容。我们从一开始就应该这样，这是一个我后悔的错误。如果机器人使用其他人的电子邮件创建帐户，受害者会收到一封电子邮件，如果他们忽略它，事情就结束了。欢迎电子邮件及其后的所有内容仅在用户验证后才会触发。 （对于通过 Google 或 GitHub 进行的社交身份验证注册，我们会立即发送欢迎电子邮件，因为这些提供商已经验证了他们的电子邮件地址。） 总结 我们可能会看到每小时 1-2 次注册，然后耸耸肩，因为对我们的业务影响基本上为零。但这些是真实的人的电子邮件地址，我们的服务正在被用来对付他们。我想向那些地址被使用的人道歉，但伸出援手只会让已经超载的收件箱中又多一封不需要的电子邮件。我们能做的最好的事情就是让它停止，并确保我们不会再被这样利用。我们还添加了更好的报告，以便下次更快地发现类似的模式。总的来说，我认为我们的反应是合理且相当迅速的，但我们应该从一开始就采取这些缓解措施，吸取教训。

Original Summary

  1. A couple of weeks ago, we noticed something odd on Suga. New users were signing up but not doing anything, they weren’t creating an org, a project, or a deployment, they just left an account sitting there. Most new users interact with the product pretty quickly, and we report on activity stats to try and understand blockers, so even a small spike in completely inactive accounts stood out. Then we looked at the names of the new users and they were entries like PfVQXvYTXjwSbEeJBjXYy and xXzMafkbPLjOaGgDaOGZjLx. We checked Resend (our email service) and could see welcome emails going out and being delivered to these accounts. They were real email addresses, with garbage names… something was off.
  2. What is subscription bombing? Subscription bombing is an attack where someone uses bots to sign up a victim’s email address across hundreds or thousands of websites. The goal isn’t to access those accounts on those websites, it’s to flood the victim’s inbox with so much noise that they can’t find the emails that actually matter. While the victim is drowning in “Welcome to SaaS Product!” and “Verify your email for Newsletter You Never Asked For”, the attacker is doing something else. They’re resetting the victim’s bank password, making purchases on their accounts, or signing up for credit cards in their name. The real security alerts and confirmation emails get buried by the noise. The people running these attacks are stealing money and impersonating real people or businesses. Every sign-up form on the internet that lets you enter any email address without verification is a tool they can exploit. How we spotted it It started on March 12 with a single unusual sign-up, then over the next two days we saw another 2-3 per day, which was low enough to be noise. Honestly, our first thought was that someone was pen testing our service. That’s pretty common, something we’ve experience with other products and, when it comes with responsible disclosures, something we appreciate, so it didn’t ring any alarm bells. By March 14 we had 6 sign-ups matching the pattern, and we noticed something else in PostHog. There was unusually high page views and interactions on the forgot password page. The combination of activities was enough to make us take a closer look. The pattern Each bot would sign up with a real victim’s email address and a garbage name. Then, within about 60 seconds, it would navigate to the forgot password page and request a reset. That meant each victim received three emails from us in under a minute:
  3. Verify your email address Welcome to Suga Reset your password
  4. Three emails they never asked for, from a product they may never have heard of. We were just one of potentially hundreds of sites being hit at the same time. The bots were also hitting the forgot password page with random email addresses, presumably trying to trigger reset emails for victims who were already a user. We reviewed one session in detail and the typing behaviour was interesting. The bot was entering values into form fields painfully slowly, one character at a time with up to a second between keystrokes. The gaps had randomness to them, but it was too random. Humans type in bursts, most people type a few characters quickly, pause, then type again. This was a flat distribution of delays trying to look human and failing. The timing between page navigations had the same quality of being randomised, but uniformly so. Enough variation to dodge simple bot detection, not enough to actually pass for a real person. Designed to be invisible This is what makes subscription bombing so different from the attacks most people think of. There was no spike, or flood of requests, or dramatic server load. It was just 1-2 sign-ups per hour and that’s it. The requests came from all over (India, Brazil, Romania, the US, Vietnam, Türkiye) which isn’t unusual until you compare it to typical traffic. Our real users typically navigate from specific countries with a reasonable correlation to the daytime hours of that country. The bot traffic had zero correlation between country and time of day, and that mismatch is what stood out. Rate limiting does nothing here, since you can’t really rate-limit against one request per hour. The whole point of this attack is to stay below the threshold, that’s one of the reasons I find this attack type so interesting. The damage isn’t to you Subscription bombing barely hurts the site owner, our email sender reputation wasn’t affected (the volume was too low). If we hadn’t been tracking user activation closely, we might not have noticed. Unfortunately, the damage is almost entirely to the people on the other end. Picture waking up to 200+ emails from services you’ve never heard of, you start deleting them, but they keep coming. Somewhere in that pile of garbage is a notification that matters, like someone changing your banking email address, resetting your password or ordering a new credit card in your name. The reason this attack works at all is that thousands of websites (newsletters, SaaS products, forums, e-commerce stores) let anyone enter any email address and immediately start sending emails to it. If your sign-up form sends email to an unverified address, your form is part of this. And because the damage falls on the victim, not the site owner, I suspect most people treat it as low priority to fix, which is wrong. It pollutes your user data and it makes your service an accomplice in harassing real people. What we did Firewall rules Our first move was tightening bot detection on our front-end hosting provider’s firewall. This cut the volume roughly in half, but didn’t eliminate it. It was also tricky to configure because we have legitimate non-browser traffic hitting our API (webhooks, service-to-service calls) that needed to keep working. We let it run for about 6 hours. In that time, two more sign-ups slipped through (one at the 5-hour mark, another at 6). The bots were getting past the verification step so we needed something better. Cloudflare Turnstile Cloudflare Turnstile was the fix, it’s a CAPTCHA alternative that doesn’t ask your users to solve puzzles. It analyses browser signals in the background and can be configured to only present a visible challenge when something looks off. You can set it up, so that for real users it’s invisible. We use Better Auth for authentication, and it has a built-in CAPTCHA plugin with Turnstile support. That made the implementation dead simple, just add the plugin server-side, drop the Turnstile component onto the auth pages like sign-up, sign-in and forgot password with @marsidev/react-turnstile, and pass the token through to the server via a header. The submit button stays disabled until Turnstile provides a valid token. After deploying Turnstile, the bot sign-ups stopped, so I want to specifically call out both Better Auth and Cloudflare Turnstile here. The combination made this a same-day fix. If your auth solution doesn’t support CAPTCHA out of the box, Turnstile’s API is still easy to integrate directly, but having it baked in saved us time. Limiting email to verified users only Even with Turnstile in place, we wanted to limit the damage if this happens again. We updated our email service code so that a user receives exactly one email from us (the verification email) until they click the link and prove they own the address. No welcome email, no product updates, nothing else until verification. We should have had it this way from the start, which is a mistake I regret. If a bot creates an account with someone else’s email, the victim gets one email, if they ignore it that’s the end of it. The welcome email and everything after it only fires once the user verifies. (For social auth sign-ups through Google or GitHub, we send the welcome email right away since those providers have already verified their email address.) Wrapping up We could have looked at 1-2 sign-ups per hour and shrugged, since the business impact to us was basically zero. But those were real people’s email addresses, and our service was being used against them. I’d like to apologise to the people whose addresses were used, but reaching out would just be one more unwanted email in an already overloaded inbox. The best we could do was make it stop and make sure we can’t be used this way again. We’ve also added better reporting to spot patterns like this sooner next time. Overall I think our response was reasonable and fairly rapid, but we should have had these mitigations in place from the start, lesson learned.

评论（默认中文）

pqdbr 2026/04/02 13:44
最近，我们遭受了另一种订阅轰炸：黑客使用我们的“更改信用卡”表单来“清理”数千张信用卡的列表，以查看哪些信用卡会通过并批准交易。

他从午夜一直持续到早上 7 点，因此没有人在旁观。

每个请求都会轮换 IP，因此没有速率限制器捕获它。

我们在注册表单和所有信用卡表单中都安装了 Cloudflare Turnstile。所有请求都经过 Turnstile 验证。

我们当时使用的是“隐形”设置，并在事件发生后切换回“推荐”设置，所以我不知道是否应该归咎于这种不太严格的设置。

就像 OP 一样，我们的网站 - 为了避免给用户带来额外的麻烦 - 不需要电子邮件验证，特别是因为我们发送的电子邮件很少。

我们从没想过这会这样咬我们。

他尝试的每个 CC 都会被收取 1 美元，以确认 CC 有效，然后立即退款，如果 CC 不批准这笔 1 美元的交易，则会出错，这就是他所使用的。约 2000 个请求中的 10% 已通过。

简单地添加确认电子邮件并不能解决问题：黑客使用了——尽管他并不需要——一次性电子邮件地址服务。

这是一件大事。如果您允许这种情况发生，支付处理商可能会禁止您。

Recently we suffered a different kind of subscription bombing: a hacker using our 'change credit card' form to 'clean' a list of thousands credit cards to see which ones would go through and approve transactions.

He ran the attack from midnight to 7AM, so there were no humans watching.

IPs were rotated on every single request, so no rate limiter caught it.

We had Cloudflare Turnstile installed in both the sign up form and in all credit card forms. All requests were validated by Turnstile.

We were running with the 'invisble' setting, and switched back to the 'recommended' setting after the incident, so I don't know if this less strict setting was to blame.

Just like OP, our website - to avoid the extra hassle on users - did not require e-mail validation, specially because we send very few e-mails.

We never thought this could bite us this way.

Every CC he tried was charged $1 as confirmation that the CC was valid, and then immediately refunded, erroring out if the CC did not approve this $1 transaction, and that's what he used. 10% of the ~2k requests went through.

Simply adding confirmation e-mail won't cut it: the hacker used - even tough he did not need it - disposable e-mail addresses services.

This is a big deal. Payment processors can ban you for allowing this to happen.

corbet 2026/04/02 21:41
长期以来，用于验证被盗卡号一直是一个问题。我们必须采取一些防御措施才能摆脱这些人维护的任何“简单网站”列表。 不过，我没有考虑过“换卡”路径……又花了一点时间远离我们的业务真正应该做的事情……
Being used to validate stolen card numbers has long been a problem; we've had to put in a number of defenses to fight our way off whatever list of "easy sites" these folks maintain. I hadn't thought about the "change card" path though...another bit of time spent away from what our business is really supposed to be doing...
在 HN 中查看
AndroTux 2026/04/02 16:07
我们通过引入静默块解决了这个问题。如果系统发现异常行为（例如，每个用户的付款尝试次数过多），它将不再向提供商发送付款尝试。相反，它会闲置一两秒，然后就会失败，并显示一般的“付款被拒绝”。大多数攻击者没有注意到他们被阻止，只是假设所有信用卡都是坏的。
We solved this by introducing a silent block. If the system notices unusual behavior (too many payment attempts per user, for example), it no longer sends the payment attempt to the provider. Instead, it idles for a second or two and then just fails with a generic “payment declined.” Most attackers don’t notice they’re being blocked and just assume all credit cards are bad.

Sounds like any per-user detection wouldn't have worked in this case.

the "notice unusual behavior" is the hard part

thousands of $1 charges and refunds in a 7 hour period seems unusual to me. then again, i've never run a site that received thousands of charges ever, so seeing it in a few hours would be obvious.

datsci_est_2015 2026/04/02 22:55
真心问一句，你是产品经理吗？你让我回想起所有建议使用 2-3 分支决策树来解决复杂分类问题的产品经理，因为这让他们觉得很直观。我们距离在这个主题中重塑整个欺诈检测领域仅几步之遥。
Genuinely asking, are you a product manager? You’re giving me flashbacks to all of the PMs who suggested a 2-3 branch decision tree for a complex classification problem, because that’s what struck them as intuitive. We are just a few baby steps away from reinventing the entire field of fraud detection within this thread.

dylan604 2026/04/02 23:32
先生，我讨厌这种暗示！我不会对这样的猪说谎！

很容易说每个网站都必须针对每种类型的攻击添加保护措施，但网站所有者不可能成为欺诈专家。而信用卡处理供应商 是 预计将成为欺诈专家。我想问一下，在这种情况下，哪里是实施欺诈检测的更好地方？这两个地方，谁的财务风险更大？

Sir, I resent the implication! I do not lie with such swine!

It's easy to say that every site must add protections against every single type of attack, except it's impossible for site owners to be experts in fraud. While credit card processing vendors are expected to be experts in fraud. I ask you where in this situation would be the better place to implement fraud detection? Of the two places, whose more financially at risk?

gib444 2026/04/02 14:44
哎哟。每个账户只需更改一次信用卡？

这是只有在此类事件发生后才会实施的监控级别之一。例如，整个子系统分析 - 7 小时内使用了 1000 次的变更卡功能（嗯，与规模成正比）是一个巨大的危险信号

Ouch. Just one credit card change per account?

This is one of those levels of monitoring that only gets put in place after such an event. Eg whole subsystem analysis - the change card feature being used 1000s of times (well, proportional to scale) in 7 hours is a massive red flag

Fokamul 2026/04/02 16:06
Cloudflare 和任何其他反机器人服务仅适用于那些没有意志力和知识来绕过它们的人。

JS是可以逆向的，你清楚的看到他们用什么数据点来检测。 任何东西都可以被欺骗，而且看起来就像人类的行为。

如果一切都失败了，你就把它外包给人工智能——总是印度人:D

Cloudflare and any other anti-bot service is only good against people without willpower and knowledge to bypass them.

JS can be reversed, you clearly see what data points they use for detection. Anything can be spoofed and it will look like human behavior.

And if everything fails, you outsource it to AI - Always Indian :D

在 HN 中查看
petercooper 2026/04/02 20:29
作为一家时事通讯公司，我们已经处理这个问题十多年了，因为我们做了正确的事情并进行了双重选择，其中包括在注册时向订阅者发送电子邮件。

直到几年前，知识产权声誉还是一个很好的防御手段。不良流量几乎全部来自某些国家的 IP 地址或我们可以阻止的数据中心 IP。如今，由于 VPN 的盛行，这种方法已经行不通了，因此许多合法用户似乎来自信誉不佳的 IP。

旋转门在其正常形式下是一个合理的解决方案，尽管“隐形”选项仍然让很多人通过。令人惊讶的是，另一件事是寻找“webdriver”的用法。尽管删除网络驱动程序指纹很容易，但我们发现大多数自动化尝试都懒得这样做。添加更多步骤、蜜罐（立即短期禁止 IP）等也会产生影响。它变成了一种在“瑞士奶酪模型”中堆积大量防御的游戏。

As a newsletter company, we've dealt with this for over a decade now since we do the right thing and do double opt-in which involves sending the subscriber an email on signup.

Until a few years ago, IP reputation was a good defence against this. The bad traffic almost entirely came from IP addresses in certain countries or from datacenter IPs we could block. Nowadays, that doesn't work due to the prevalence of VPNs, so many legitimate users appear to come from low reputation IPs.

Turnstile is a reasonable solution in its normal form, though the 'invisible' option still lets a lot of them through. Another thing that works, surprisingly, is looking for "webdriver" usage. Despite being easy to strip out webdriver fingerprints, we find that the majority of automated attempts do not bother to do this. Adding more steps, honeypots (with an immediate short term IP ban), etc. also have an impact. It becomes a game of piling up numerous defences in a sort of "Swiss cheese model".

在 HN 中查看
aftbit 2026/04/02 23:34
这是一个有趣的眼镜蛇效应。年龄验证（如果你采取最慈善的观点，“目的”是为了让儿童在网上更安全）迫使越来越多的人使用 VPN，这总体上降低了 IP 声誉作为信号的价值，迫使提供商接受信誉较差的 IP，因为真正的客户来自他们，这意味着提供商更容易受到可用于针对儿童的攻击。
That's a fun cobra effect. Age verification ("intended" to make children safer online, if you take the most charitable view) forces more and more people to use VPNs, which overall degrades the value of IP reputation as a signal, forcing providers to accept less reputable IPs because real customers come from them, which means that providers are more vulnerable to attacks that can be used to target children.
在 HN 中查看
observationist 2026/04/03 01:42
重点不是防止针对儿童的攻击，而是把关内容，使其远离儿童。总体而言，提供商更容易受到攻击，因为存在把关，因为不可避免地会使用 VPN 和代理等工具来绕过正在使用的机制。这种反匿名的具体目标是降低个人的安全性，使他们受到国家的监视和控制。这与“保护儿童”无关，也从来没有。

信息灾难的四骑士总是在争夺权力，他们从来都不是为了真正保护公民、儿童，或者保护一个国家或地区。

The point isn't protection from attacks that target children, it's gatekeeping content to keep it away from children. Providers are more vulnerable to attacks, overall, because of that gatekeeping, because of ht inevitable use of tools like VPNs and proxies to bypass the mechanisms being used. This sort of anti-anonymity is specifically and precisely targeted at decreasing the security of individuals, subjecting them to surveillance and control by the state. It has nothing to do with "protecting the children" and never did.

The four horsemen of the infocalypse are always about power grabs, they're never about actually protecting citizens, or children, or securing a country or region.

在 HN 中查看
HexDecOctBin 2026/04/02 13:39
几个月前我就受到了这样的攻击。我为每个帐户（模式为product@example.com）使用不同的电子邮件地址，并为 Git 提交使用单独的地址（如 git@example.com）。正是第二封邮件遭到攻击，我在 12 小时内收到了大约 500 封电子邮件。幸运的是，由于我不希望有人真正通过 Git 地址向我发送电子邮件，因此我只是设置了一个过滤器，将它们全部发送到一个单独的文件夹，以便在闲暇时查看。

12 小时后，电子邮件的速度停止了，然后我开始收到在同一域上伪造美国政治性质地址的电子邮件（我启用了通配符别名），这表明有人可能试图发泄一些不满。这种情况只持续了大约半个小时，袭击者似乎已经放弃并停止了。

奇怪的是，在攻击期间我没有收到攻击者可能试图隐藏的任何电子邮件。这首先让我对这次攻击的目的感到困惑。

I was attacked in this way a couple of months back. I use a different email address for each account (of the pattern product@example.com), and use a separate address for Git commits (like git@example.com). It was this second one that was attacked and I ended up with some 500 emails within 12 hours. Fortunately, since I don't expect anyone to actually email me on the Git address, I just put up a filter to send them all to a separate folder to go over at my leisure.

After 12 hours, the pace of emails came to a halt, and then I started receiving emails to made up addresses of a American political nature on the same domain (I have wildcard alias enabled), suggesting that someone was perhaps trying to vent some frustration. This only lasted for about half an hour before the attacker seems to have given up and stopped.

Strangely, I didn't receive any email during the attack which the attacker might have been trying to hide. Which has left me confused at to the purpose of this attack in the first place.

在 HN 中查看
chicagojoe 2026/04/02 14:04
我最近也遇到过这种情况，也没有掩盖任何电子邮件活动（我梳理了 3000 多封垃圾邮件）。

请仔细检查您的收件箱中是否添加了转发规则，并添加了一些针对 SIM 卡交换的保护措施。

就我而言，他们没有损害我的任何帐户，但确实尝试开立一张新信用卡，因此值得仔细检查您的信用报告。

I had this happen recently too, also not covering up any email activity (I combed through 3000+ spam emails).

Double check that there are no forwarding rules added to your inbox and add some protection against a SIM swap.

In my case, they didn't compromise any of my accounts but did attempt to open a new credit card so it would be worth double checking your credit reports.

在 HN 中查看
m132 2026/04/02 13:09
这是一个问题，但我真的不喜欢这个解决方案。将存在已知安全问题的网站置于 Cloudflare 的 Turnstile 后面就像强制执行代码签名一样，一直有效，直到不起作用为止，同时有助于将权力集中在单个法律实体周围，同时激怒合法用户。

互联网经过精心设计，旨在抵御核战争，而这种被广泛采用的方法正在慢慢地将其变成以前的影子。尽管去年发生了 us-east1 和多次 Cloudflare 中断，但我们仍然对此视而不见，甚至将其合理化为一件好事，因为这样，如果我们失败了，那么我们的竞争对手也会失败......

It's a problem, but I really dislike the solution. Putting a website with known security issues behind Cloudflare's Turnstile is comparable to enforcing code signing—works until it doesn't, and in the meantime, helps centralize power around a single legal entitiy while pissing legitimate users off.

The Internet was carefully designed to withstand a nuclear war and this approach, being adopted en masse, is slowly turning it into a shadow of its former self. And despite the us-east1 and multiple Cloudflare outages of last year, we continue to stay blind to this or even rationalize it as a good thing, because that way if we're down, then so are our competitors...

在 HN 中查看
pverheggen 2026/04/02 14:00
我不会将此称为“已知的安全问题”，这是任何注册或忘记密码页面的固有问题。

另外，我怀疑这会惹恼用户，因为他们在不可见模式下添加了 Turnstile，并有选择地添加到身份验证流程中的某些页面。 即使服务关闭，已登录的用户也不会受到影响。 这与 Reddit 等使用站点范围的机器人保护的网站有很大不同，后者会创建这些插页式验证码页面。

I wouldn't call this "known security issues", it's an inherent problem with any signup or forgot password page.

Also, I doubt this is going to be pissing users off since they added Turnstile in invisible mode, and selectively to certain pages in the auth flow. Already signed in users will not be affected, even if the service is down. This is way different from sites like Reddit who use their site-wide bot protection, which creates those interstitial captcha pages.

在 HN 中查看
jijijijij 2026/04/02 19:43
> 我不会将此称为“已知的安全问题”，这是任何注册或忘记密码页面的固有问题。

但这并不是与生俱来的！简单、明确的修复：颠倒通信关系。如果用户无论如何都必须打开他们的邮件应用程序，您可以简单地要求他们向您发送电子邮件，而不是反之亦然。这会 解决 问题彻底解决了。 （如果可以可靠地欺骗发件人，那么该服务一开始就不会涉及。）

现在，它会稍微增加摩擦并降低便利性。这就是为什么它没有完成。它本质上与黑暗模式、数据收集和可疑的新用户获取不兼容，但这也可以通过标准和集成来解决 - 而不需要 Cloudflare 事实上的基础设施！

可能的方便、更好的解决方案：让浏览器发送此邮件，或者通过将模板传递给邮件应用程序，将 SMTP 集成到浏览器/插件中，或者设置一种新颖的身份验证协议，这实际上可能完全消除人类交互。

就好像 2FA 安全性是索要电子邮件和/或电话的主要动机一样。公司希望尽快获得用户ID（如果可能的话UID），以增加用户数据价值并获得营销机会。在发送表格之前，我曾经在输入地址后收到一封“欢迎邮件”。是的...

> I wouldn't call this "known security issues", it's an inherent problem with any signup or forgot password page.

It's not inherent, though! Easy, definite fix: Reverse the communication relation. If the user has to open their mail app anyway, you could simply require them to send an email to you, instead of vice versa. This would solve the problem completely. (If spoofing the sender could be done reliably, the service wouldn't be involved in the first place.)

Now, it would slightly increase friction and lower convenience. That's why it's not done. It's inherently incompatible with dark patterns, data collection and questionable new user acquisition, but this too could be solved through standards and integration - without making Cloudflare de facto infrastructure necessity!

Possible convenient, better solutions: Have the browser send this mail, either by passing a template to the mail app, integrating SMTP into the browser/addon, or instate a novel authentication protocol, which in fact may remove the human interaction completely.

As if 2FA security was the main motivation for asking for email, and/or phone anyway. Companies want user IDs, if possible UIDs, as soon as possible to increase user data value and gain marketing opportunities. I once had a "welcome mail" after typing in the address, before sending the form. Yeah...

Nothing with email can ever be an easy fix, although the idea is amusing. It is inherently the problem.
在 HN 中查看
jijijijij 2026/04/02 21:33
“固有”有一种绝对性，我反驳了这一点。我同意，依赖电子邮件本身就很麻烦。

但正如我所说，这不是技术上或道德上的强制要求，而是如何确保用户不会生气（被邮件轰炸是糟糕的公关）。公司首先为其（未来的）股东收集所有这些 ID。询问电子邮件不会提醒人们。电话号码会更令人震惊，但这仍然成为常态。他们也会要求您提供护照照片，但是......哦，等等！

随意将 Cloudflare 集成到所有内容中（包括 TLS 终止，哈哈），只会让数据收集激励更大。我们不要因为宣布 Cloudflare 是基本必需品而屈服。或者这样做，但不要抱怨你作为牛的不被承认的生活。

'Inherent' has an absoluteness, which I disproved. Relying on email, is inherently troublesome, I agree.

But as I said, it's not about what's technically, or ethically mandated, but what's ensuring users won't get annoyed (getting bombed with mails is bad PR). Companies collect all these IDs for their (future) shareholders first and foremost. Asking for email doesn't alert people. Phone number would be more alarming, but that's still becoming the norm. They would ask for a picture of your passport too, but ... oh, wait!

Casually integrating Cloudflare into everything (incl. TLS termination lol), only makes data collection incentives greater. Let's not give in by declaring Cloudflare a fundamental necessity. Or do, but don't complaint about your disowned life as cattle.

So your solution would be to do nothing?

Cloudflare is an excellent solution for many things. The internet was designed to withstand a nuclear war, but it also wasn’t designed for the level of hostility that goes on on the internet these days.

nmcfarl 2026/04/02 20:57
但 cloudflare 也很困难，我在 Starlink（因为我唯一的其他选择是 Hughes net），而我选择的浏览器是 Safari。 没有VPN，只有无聊的广告拦截器。

我经常被 Cloudflare 阻止查看东西，偶尔也会被阻止购买东西。就在这个周末，这是价值 100 美元的运动服。我只是不断点击该框，但它永远不会让我完成购买。第七次或第十次之后，我去找另一个真正愿意卖给我的供应商。 我比平时更生气，因为此时该网站已经有了我的信用卡——但正如本文所证明的那样，即使使用信用卡，也有理由阻止订单。

But cloudflare is also just difficult, I’m on Starlink (because where I am my only other option is Hughes net), and my browser of choice is Safari. No vpn, and only boring ad blockers.

I routinely blocked by Cloudflare from viewing things and occasionally, I am blocked from buying things. Just this weekend, it was $100 worth of athletic wear. I just keep clicking the box and it never lets me complete the purchase. After the 7th or 10th time I go and find another vendor that would actually sell to me. I was more annoyed than usual because the website already had my credit card at this point – but as this article proves there are reasons to block an order even with a credit card.

stingraycharles 2026/04/02 15:49

在 HN 中查看
sarchertech 2026/04/02 18:18
你必须认真思考问题并应用单独的解决方案。无论如何，Cloudflare 对作者来说不起作用。即使他们启用了更多侵入性设置，它也只会添加验证码，这不太可能阻止这个特定的攻击者（无论如何你都可以自己轻松完成）。

在这种情况下，我假设攻击者使用更改信用卡表单的原因是因为添加信用卡的唯一其他方法是在注册时向您的卡收取订阅费（金额远大于 1 美元）。

因此，解决方案是不要向尚未存档有效（有效）卡的客户显示更改卡选项。

一个更通用的解决方案是对任何允许某人向信用卡收取极小金额的内容进行站点范围的速率限制。

或者更好的是，没有任何方法可以向卡收取极少量的费用。 检查新卡时保留 150 美元而不是 1 美元

不过，就 cloudflare 集中化而言，您无法通过吸引个体开发人员变得更聪明并做更多工作来解决这个问题。这需要监管。这是一个弹性和国家安全问题，我们不希望任何一家公司充当互联网看门人。但多年来我对谷歌也说过同样的话。

You have to think hard about the problem and apply individual solutions. Cloudflare didn’t work for the author anyway. Even if they had more intrusive settings enabled it would have just added captchas, which wouldn’t likely have stopped this particular attacker (and you can do on your own easily anyway).

In this case I assume the reason the attacker used the change credit card form was because the only other way to add a credit card is when signing up, which charges your card the subscription fee (a much larger amount than $1).

So the solution is don’t show the change card option to customers who don’t already have an active (valid) card on file.

A more generic solution is site wide rate limiting for anything that allows someone to charge very small amounts to a credit card.

Or better yet don’t have any way to charge very small amounts to cards. Do a $150 hold instead of $1 when checking a new card

As far as cloudflare centralization goes though, you’re not going to solve this problem by appealing to individual developers to be smarter and do more work. It’s going to take regulation. It’s a resiliency and national security issue, we don’t want a single company to function as the internet gatekeeper. But I’ve said the same about Google for years.

在 HN 中查看
HumanOstrich 2026/04/02 18:58
在这种情况下，你的解决方案似乎都没有用，尤其是 150 美元的保留。支付处理的全站速率限制？太复杂，维护成本高，而且容易搞砸。

您无法 100% 阻止这些尝试，但您可以通过检查尝试更改银行卡的基本信息来阻止一大类尝试，就像它们都有不同的名称和邮政编码一样。将其与其他（有用的）缓解措施结合起来。也许会收到一条警报，表明在过去的几个小时甚至几天内，一群用户的 90% 的换卡尝试都失败了。

None of your solutions seem useful in this case, especially a $150 hold. Site-wide rate limiting for payment processing? Too complicated, high-maintenance, and easy to mess up.

You can't block 100% of these attempts, but you can block a large class of them by checking basic info for the attempted card changes like they all have different names and zip codes. Combine that with other (useful) mitigations. Maybe getting an alert that in the past few hours or days even, 90% of card change attempts have failed for a cluster of users.

在 HN 中查看
sarchertech 2026/04/03 04:52
>在这种情况下，你的解决方案似乎都没有用，尤其是 150 美元的保留。

攻击者正在追捕小额指控。这就是他们首先追捕这些人的原因。

> 支付处理的全站速率限制？太复杂，维护成本高，而且容易搞砸。

然后你给出的解决方案复杂 10 倍，维护成本高，而且容易搞砸。

>您无法 100% 阻止这些尝试，但您可以通过检查尝试更改银行卡的基本信息来阻止其中一大部分，就像它们都有不同的名称和邮政编码一样。

这本质上是速率限制的更复杂的超集。

>None of your solutions seem useful in this case, especially a $150 hold.

Attackers are going after small charges. That's the reason they're going after these guys in the first place.

>Site-wide rate limiting for payment processing? Too complicated, high-maintenance, and easy to mess up.

And then you give a solution that is 10x as complicated, high maintenance, and easy to mess up.

>You can't block 100% of these attempts, but you can block a large class of them by checking basic info for the attempted card changes like they all have different names and zip codes.

This is essentially a much more complex superset of rate limiting.

withinboredom 2026/04/02 21:14
受害者显然会注意到 150 美元的保留，因此攻击者甚至不会尝试。
A $150 hold would clearly be noticed by the victim, so the attacker wouldn't even try it.
在 HN 中查看
dpkirchner 2026/04/02 21:58
也许如果我的银行给我发了电子邮件，否则我会怀疑。当地的加油站通常使用 200 美元的保留费，我必须竭尽全力才能看到​​它发生。
Maybe if my bank emailed me, otherwise I doubt it. Local gas stations routinely use $200 holds and I'd have to go way out of my way to see it happen.
在 HN 中查看
sarchertech 2026/04/03 04:54
关键是是否每个用户都真正注意到了这一点，因为有足够多的用户注意到了这一点，以至于攻击者专门寻找进行小额收费的能力。如果您删除该功能，他们就会寻找其他地方。
The point is whether every user actually notices it, it's that enough of them do that attackers are specifically looking for the ability to do small charges. If you remove that capability, they will look elsewhere.
在 HN 中查看
scott_w 2026/04/02 22:54
是的……不，不会。我曾见过用户的银行账户（意外地）被清空，因为他们不断刷新。区区 150 英镑是不会被注册的，直到为时已晚。
Yeah… no it wouldn’t. I’ve watched users have their bank accounts emptied (by accident) because they kept refreshing. A measly £150 isn’t going to register until it’s too late anyway.
在 HN 中查看
sarchertech 2026/04/03 04:55
攻击者利用任何允许他们进行小额收费的网站是有原因的，因为足够多的用户会注意到更大的收费。

无论每个用户是否注意到，攻击者都在寻找进行小额收费的能力，如果您删除该功能，他们就会继续前进。

There's a reason attackers exploit any site that lets them do small charges, it's because enough users will notice a larger charge.

Whether every user notices it or not, attackers are looking for the ability to do small charges, and if you remove that they'll move on.

RobotToaster 2026/04/02 14:54

在 HN 中查看
cuu508 2026/04/02 13:42
注册流程怎么样 用户 发送第一封电子邮件？他们向 signups@example.com（或生成的唯一地址）发送一封电子邮件，并在回复中收到一次性登录链接。不过，该服务必须小心，不要处理欺骗性电子邮件。

另一种方法是根本不要求提供电子邮件地址，例如 HN。

How about a signup flow where the user sends the first email? They send an email to signups@example.com (or to a generated unique address), and receive a one-time sign-in link in the reply. The service would have to be careful not to process spoofed emails though.

Another approach is to not ask for an email address at all, like here on HN.

__david__ 2026/04/02 15:51

> how do you "be careful" with spoofed email?

habinero 2026/04/02 21:55
天啊。告诉我你在现实生活中从来没有在不告诉我的情况下处理过这些事情，哈哈

通常，你在现实生活中能做的最好的事情就是“可能很好”或“也许不太好”，但这还不足以证明阻止客户是合理的。电子邮件是一项古老的技术，但有一个 很多 野外的变异。

Oh god. Tell me you've never dealt with those in real life without telling me lol

Usually the very best you can do IRL is "probably fine" or "maybe not fine" and that's just not good enough to justify blocking customers. Email is an old tech and there's a lot of variation in the wild.

在 HN 中查看
red_admiral 2026/04/02 16:04
可悲的是，这就是你的转化率降到最低的原因。

注册表单中的每一个额外字段都会降低转化率。

That is how you get your conversion rate to drop to the floor, sadly.

Every extra field in the sign-up form already lowers the conversion rate.

在 HN 中查看
xwowsersx 2026/04/02 14:08
乍一看这听起来很吸引人，因为它颠覆了信任模型......而不是服务发起联系，而是用户预先证明了对电子邮件的控制这感觉更干净，并且可以说对某些类别的滥用更强大

但从用户体验的角度来看，这是不可能的

您要求用户

- 离开网站/应用程序

- 打开他们的电子邮件客户端

- 撰写消息或至少点击发送

- 等待回复

- 然后回来继续

与输入电子邮件 -> 单击链接相比，这需要很多步骤。每个额外的步骤都是一个下降点，特别是对于移动设备或技术含量较低的用户而言。许多人甚至不再设置传统的邮件客户端，他们依赖网络邮件或应用程序切换，这增加了更多的摩擦

它还引入了歧义

- 我到底应该发送什么

- 有效果吗

- 如果我没有收到回复怎么办

从服务方面来看，您正在用一个简单的、易于理解的流程来换取一个更复杂的入站电子邮件处理系统，其中包含所有常见的令人头痛的问题（欺骗解析传递延迟垃圾邮件过滤）

在实践中，大多数系统都会优化以最大限度地减少用户的工作量，即使这意味着接受一定程度的滥用并在其他地方减轻它。显着增加摩擦的解决方案......无论原则如何......都不会被广泛采用

因此，虽然从协议设计的角度来看这个想法很有趣，但很难看到它在与真实用户的接触中幸存下来

It sounds appealing at first because it flips the trust model... instead of the service initiating contact the user proves control of their email up front That feels cleaner and arguably more robust against certain classes of abuse

But from a UX standpoint its a nonstarter

Youre asking users to

- leave the site/app

- open their email client

- compose a message or at least hit send

- wait for a reply

- then come back and continue

Thats a lot of steps compared to enter email -> click link. Each additional step is a dropoff point especially on mobile or for less technical users. Many people dont even have a traditional mail client set up anymore, they rely on webmail or app switching which adds even more friction

It also introduces ambiguity

- What exactly am I supposed to send

- did it work

- What if I dont get a reply

From the service side youre trading a simple well understood flow for a much more complex inbound email processing system with all the usual headaches (spoofing parsing delivery delays spam filtering)

In practice most systems optimize for minimizing user effort even if that means accepting some level of abuse and mitigating it elsewhere. A solution that significantly increases friction... no matter how principled...just wont get adopted widely

So while the idea is interesting from a protocol design perspective its hard to see it surviving contact with real users

在 HN 中查看
cuu508 2026/04/02 17:09
我认为主要的用户体验障碍是 陌生 – 目前没有人进行这样的注册。但如果您使用“mailto:”链接，流程并不需要那么糟糕。在幸福的情况下：

- 用户点击链接

- 他们的电子邮件客户端打开，其中已预先填写“收件人：”、“主题：”、“正文：”字段

- 用户点击“发送”

- 几秒钟后，登录链接到达他们的收件箱

I think the main UX obstacle is that it is unfamiliar – no-one does signups like that currently. But the flow does not need to be quite as bad, if you use "mailto:" links. In the happy case:

- user click on the link

- their email client opens, with the To:, Subject:, Body: fields pre-filled

- user clicks "Send"

- a few seconds later a sign-in link arrives in their inbox

在 HN 中查看
__david__ 2026/04/02 17:09
> 但从用户体验的角度来看，这是不可能的

不同意。用户体验将非常相似。单击 mailto 链接，打开电子邮件客户端，其中包含预先编写的收件人、主题和正文。单击发送。服务器接收邮件，网页继续/完成注册过程。无需电子邮件回复。 这是不同的，但并不疯狂。

> But from a UX standpoint its a nonstarter

Disagree. The UX would be pretty similar. Click a mailto link which opens the email client with to, subject and body precomposed. Click send. Server receives mail and the web page continues/finishes the sign up process. No need for an email reply. It’s different, but it’s not crazy.

在 HN 中查看
grufkork 2026/04/02 14:56
在所有年龄验证和机器人垃圾邮件不断发生的情况下，匿名私钥/公钥身份证明可以发挥作用：新注册的服务必须通过邮件服务器的质询，以证明用户确实打算注册。尽管我猜想这与用户服务器发起通信基本上是相同的。实际上，仅显示已知发件人的激进白名单/垃圾邮件过滤器也可以解决此问题，但据我了解，部分攻击已经损害了目标的邮件服务。拥有第三个解耦身份提供商可以解决这个问题，但随后这就会成为单点故障......
Amidst all the age verification and bot spam going on, anonymous private/public key proof of identity could work: the newly signed up service must pass a challenge from the mail server to prove the user actually intended to sign up. Though I guess that would be basically the same thing as the users server initiating the communication. Really, just an aggressive whitelist/spam filter that only shows known senders solves it too, but as I understand part of the attack is having already compromised the mail service of the target. Having a third decoupled identity provider would resolve that, but then that becomes a single point of failure…

AussieWog93 2026/04/02 13:19
老实说，我真的很喜欢 CloudFlare 作为一项业务。 没有供应商锁定，只有真正的好产品。

如果他们稍后转身做坏事，我所需要做的就是将名称服务器更改为竞争对手，而我网站的用户甚至不会注意到。

If they turn around later and do something evil, literally all I need to do is change the nameserver to a competitor and the users of my website won't even notice.

在 HN 中查看
AndroTux 2026/04/02 16:30
那么，除了 DNS 之外，您不会使用他们的任何服务，此时您根本不需要使用 Cloudflare。

一旦您打开他们提供的任何其他服务，您就需要主动迁移。这是真正提供好处的服务的固有问题。如果您说“我可以迁移到任何其他名称服务器”，那么您首先是在告诉我 Cloudflare 没有用处。因为如果你这样做了，你就不能不再使用它了。

假设您正在使用他们的 WAF。当然，您只需更改域的名称服务器即可迁移。但现在您不再拥有 WAF。他们的 CDN 也是如此。或者他们的负载平衡器。或者他们的对象存储。或者他们的验证码。

Then you're not using any of their services besides DNS, at which point you don't need to use Cloudflare at all.

As soon as you turn on any other service they offer, you need to actively migrate away. It's an inherent issue of services that actually provide a benefit. If you're saying "I can just migrate to any other nameserver" then you're telling me you have no use for Cloudflare in the first place. Because if you did, you couldn't just not use it anymore.

Let's say you're using their WAF. Sure, you can just change your domain's nameserver and you've migrated away. But now you no longer have a WAF. Same for their CDN. Or their load balancer. Or their object storage. Or their CAPTCHAs.

在 HN 中查看
thisisnow 2026/04/02 16:55
我认为当您从他们那里购买域名时，他们也会将您锁定在他们的 DNS 中，这与其他允许自由更改您的 NS 的注册商不同。当然，您可以以较低的价格将域名转移到其他地方，但关键是他们会付出额外的努力来强制他们的 NS，这是我在其他注册商那里没有见过的。
I think they also lock you into their DNS when you buy a domain from them, unlike other registrars who allow to change your NS freely. Sure, you can just transfer the domain elsewhere for a small price, but the point is they go the extra mile to force their NS, which I havent seen with other registrars.

在 HN 中查看
jb1991 2026/04/02 14:50
在当今这个时代，我一直不明白的一件事是，世界上有这么多公司，包括像银行这样处理机密数据的公司，为什么不要求用户在输入电子邮件地址后验证其地址。不幸的是，我有一个非常通用的电子邮件地址，很容易输入错误，而且我几乎每天都会收到昂贵的度假酒店的订单收据、银行转账或电汇确认信，这是一长串我不应该收到的东西，因为发送这些电子邮件的公司从未让用户验证他们是否输入了正确的电子邮件地址。它们是合法的电子邮件，通常会发送给与我同名但姓氏不同的人，因此该人只是不小心输入了错误的电子邮件地址。

让我感到疯狂的是，为这些公司工作的开发人员从未想过实施简单的电子邮件验证。

One thing I have never understood in this current age is how in the world so many companies, including ones that handle confidential data like banks, don’t require a user to verify their email address after it’s entered. I have an unfortunately very generic email address that’s easy to mistype, and I am almost every day receiving order receipts for expensive vacation hotels, bank transfer or wire transfer confirmations, a very long list of things that I should not be receiving simply because the companies sending those emails never had the user verify if they entered the right email address. They are legitimate emails, they are often addressed to someone with the same first name as me but a different last name, so that person simply typed the wrong email address accidentally.

It’s bonkers to me that there’s any developers out there working for these companies that never thought to implement simple email verification.

在 HN 中查看
letier 2026/04/02 15:01
我有一个很早的 Gmail 地址。一个非常常见的名字加两个字母。现在几乎已经无法使用了。发票、订阅、有关某些人房地产交易的重要文件。它们最终都会出现在我的收件箱中。

我附有大约 20 或 30 个 Google 帐户，其中我是备用电子邮件地址。这些人忘记了密码或停止使用他们的帐户，我会收到有关此情况的电子邮件通知。无需我方确认。

我设置了一个不太可能出现此问题的新地址。但从旧的迁移过来并不容易……

I have a very early gmail address. A very common first name plus two letters. It is almost unusable by now. Invoices, subscriptions, important documents about some persons real estate dealings. They all end up in my inbox.

I have around 20 or 30 google accounts attached where i am the backup email address. Those people forget their passwords or stop using their accounts and i get email notifications about that. No confirmation from my side necessary.

I set up a new address that is less likely to end up with this problem. But migrating away from the old one is not easy…

pixelesque 2026/04/02 15:14
就gmail地址而言，情况与我完全相同（尽管我的名字不太常见）。

我明白了 这么多 其他 $MY_NAME 电子邮件，包括账单（包括多张信用卡和 Afterpay 等）、送货、医疗详细信息/报告、家庭通讯等。

很明显，相当多的在线服务公然不验证电子邮件地址，他们只是假设电子邮件是有效的并允许人们开始使用它。

Exactly the same situation with me in terms of gmail address (although my names are less common).

I get so many other $MY_NAME emails, including bills (including multiple credit cards and things like Afterpay), deliveries, medical details/reports, family communications, etc, etc.

And it's very clear that quite a few online services blatantly don't verify email addresses, they just assume the email is valid and allow the person to start using it.

Gigachad 2026/04/02 14:53
因为确认电子邮件会带来摩擦。每个人都在针对低摩擦进行优化，即使这存在私人数据泄露的风险，您总是可以将其归咎于用户输入错误的电子邮件。
Because confirming the email introduces friction. And everyone is optimising for low friction even if it risks private data leaks, which you can always blame on the user for typing their email wrong.

ProllyInfamous 2026/04/02 23:57
>令我感到疯狂的是，为这些公司工作的开发人员从未想过实施简单的电子邮件验证

其他 POV：我只是 不要使用电子邮件 - 喜欢 根本不 （甚至不再有垃圾邮件帐户） - 如果您的网站要求我输入电子邮件地址 只是为了买东西， 我 将要 找到允许交易脚本继续进行的刻录/临时电子邮件（“10 分钟电子邮件”）。

当商店允许我继续进行时，我个人非常感激 宾客交易[即非登录、非邮件购买] - 即使这意味着如果交易被标记/延迟我可能需要“打电话”[通常不是]。

故意 不举例子 为了避免黑客/目标，但许多美国服装制造商提供这些无摩擦（且合法）的购买途径。

>It’s bonkers to me that there’s any developers out there working for these companies that never thought to implement simple email verification

Other POV: I simply do not use email — like at all (don't even have a SPAM account anymore) — if your website requires me to enter an email address just to buy something, I WILL find a burner/temporary email that will allow the transaction script to proceed ("10 minute email").

I am personally grateful when a store allows me to proceed with a guest transaction [i.e. non-login, non-email purchase] — even if that means I might need to "call in" should the transaction be flagged/delayed [usually isn't].

Intentionally not giving examples to avoid hacker/targetting, but many US clothing manufacturers offer these frictionless (and legitimate) purchase pathways.

cestith 2026/04/02 23:18
我一直收到这些东西。有一次，我收到了有人要求提供的信息，内容涉及一家制药公司帮助支付低收入个人（显然是输错电子邮件地址的人）的药物费用。其中包括他的姓名、城市和其他一些个人信息。该药物仅被批准用于一个医疗问题。完全违反 HIPAA/HITECH。

他们没有 HIPAA 协调员的头衔，所以我必须向他们的法律部门解释如何避免这种情况。

I get this stuff all the time. Once, I got the information someone requested about a drug company helping cover the cost of a drug for a lower-income individual (who was apparently the one who mistyped his email address). It included his name, city, and some other personal information. The drug was only approved for one medical issue. Total HIPAA/HITECH violation.

They didn’t have a HIPAA coordinator by title, so I got to explain how to avoid this to their legal department.

在 HN 中查看
pprotas 2026/04/02 14:55
这是故意的。电子邮件验证是一种摩擦，因此它让用户有机会重新考虑他们的购买是否真的有必要。这对生意不利，因为他们更喜欢你冲动。

此外，人们通常会正确输入电子邮件，尤其是现在有自动填充功能。因此，不发送确认电子邮件是对快乐路径的优化。

This is intentional. Email verification is friction, so it gives users a chance to reconsider whether their purchase is really necessary. This is bad for business, because they’d prefer if you were impulsive.

Also, people usually type their emails correctly, especially these days with auto-fill. So not sending confirmation emails is optimizing for the happy path.

在 HN 中查看
jb1991 2026/04/02 15:05
不仅仅是谈论购买。我收到包含世界各地电汇银行号码的交易详细信息。这太荒谬了。

有一次，一位律师甚至将法庭案件的所有法律程序发送到了错误的地址。

Not just talking about purchases. I receive transaction details with bank numbers for wire transfers around the world. It’s ridiculous.

I was once even sent all of the legal proceedings for a court case by a lawyer who was sent to the wrong address.

绝对 100% 防欺诈和安全证明。

nobodywillobsrv 2026/04/02 14:57
是的，这太疯狂了。我也有同样的情况，收到了抵押贷款申请、警察详细信息、警察工作申请、按摩收据等等。其中许多将被视为重要的客户数据泄露。

我什至收到过创始人级别的电子邮件，这些电子邮件可能是机密的，因为我和科技界的某个人的名字一样。

当显然是某个真人在管理一个小团体时，我会回复或报告，但对于大型整体来说，除了快速回复公司电子邮件之外，几乎没有什么可做的。

真的希望有一些关于为非恶意错误的同名错误的特定问题构建一些东西的高层讨论。

谷歌可以做到这一点，但在我看来，这不是一种可以在他们关心的规模上货币化的东西，而且我无法想出一种方法让这项工作在电子邮件巨石之外运行。

很想听听是否有人有想法。

Yes it is insane. I am in same boat and have received mortgage applications, police details, applications for police jobs, massage receipts you name it. Many would be considered important leaks of customer data.

I have even had founder level emails that presumably are confidential sent to me because I share the name of someone operating in tech.

I respond or report when it's obviously some real person running a small group but for large monoliths there is very little to do except quickly reply to corporate email.

Really wish there was some kind of high level discussion about building something for this specific problem of non malicious wrong person same name errors.

Google could do it it's just not something that is monetizable at a scale they care about IMO and I have not been able to think of a way to make this work operating outside of email monoliths.

Would love to hear if anyone has ideas.

fragmede 2026/04/02 15:24
什么谷歌 有 完成后，就是为用户添加个人资料图片，所以如果我发送电子邮件至girlfriend@gmail.com，我会收到她的照片，但如果我发送电子邮件至giirlfriend@gmail.com，我会看到其他人的pfp，这足以让我意识到我拼写错误。我确信他们还可以做更多的事情，但他们至少意识到了这个问题。
What Google has done, is add profile pictures for users, so if I'm emailing girlfriend@gmail.com I get her picture, but if I email giirlfriend@gmail.com, I see someone else's pfp which is enough to get me to realize I've spelled it wrong. I'm sure there's more they could be doing, but they're aware of the problem at least.

mfi 2026/04/02 14:50
我在电子邮件安全公司 xorlab[0] 工作，我和同事对我们在客户身上看到的真实订阅/电子邮件轰炸浪潮进行了彻底分析[1]。

以下是我们分析的攻击中的一些有趣的附加信息：

* 电子邮件轰炸是一种服务，您可以花 10 美元购买 10,000 个积分，每小时可以轻松轰炸 2000 多封电子邮件，轰炸目标收件箱。

* 大多数电子邮件轰炸攻击都在早上 8 点至 10 点之间开始。

* 最常见的攻击日是星期五

[0] https://www.xorlab.com/en/

[1] https://www.xorlab.com/en/blog/from-chaos-to-control-insight...

I work the email security company xorlab[0], where my colleagues and I did a thorough analysis of real subscription/email bombing waves that we saw at our customers[1].

Here are some interesting additional information from the attacks we analyzed:

* Email bombing as a service is a thing, where you can buy 10,000 credits for $10 and easily bomb target inboxes with over 2000 emails per hour.

* Most all email bombing attacks starts in the morning, between 8-10.

* Most common day of attack is Friday

[0] https://www.xorlab.com/en/

[1] https://www.xorlab.com/en/blog/from-chaos-to-control-insight...

在 HN 中查看
znnajdla 2026/04/02 13:33
我绝对拒绝使用 BigTech 看门人或无用的验证码（任何足够先进的机器人都可以绕过任何验证码）。我们在启动时通过一个简单的 LLM 过滤器运行名称来解决这个问题 - 如果名称是像 Px2846skxojw 这样的乱码，只需阻止注册。效果出奇的好。当然，如果机器人知道你在做什么，这很容易解决。但机器人会寻找简单的目标，只要互联网上有足够多的振动编码垃圾目标，它们就不会费心绕过精心设计的应用程序。
I absolutely refuse to use BigTech gatekeepers or useless CAPTCHAS (any sufficiently advanced bot can get around any CAPTCHA anyway). We solved this at our startup by running names through a simple LLM filter - if the name is gibberish like Px2846skxojw just block the signup. Worked surprisingly well. Of course this is easy to get around if the bot knows what you’re doing. But bots look for easy targets, as long as there are enough vibe coded crap targets on the internet they’re not going to bother with circumventing a carefully designed app.

在 HN 中查看
stanac 2026/04/02 16:24
我在其中一张表单上添加了自定义验证码（简单的数学运算，即稍微扭曲的图片和音频替代品），它可以防止大约 80% 的垃圾邮件提交。不到 1% 的垃圾邮件通过，其他约 20% 的关键词被阻止（如“性”、“激情”等）。
I added custom captcha (simple math as slightly distorted pictures with audio alternative) on one of my forms, it prevents ~80% of spam submissions. less than 1% of spam passes, other ~20% are blocked on key words (like "sex", "passion", etc...).

在 HN 中查看
imiric 2026/04/02 14:09
那么您的解决方案是部署一个黑匣子，可以使用单个字段的基本查找表来解决？

验证码从来就不是在所有情况下 100% 有效的，也不是唯一的安全解决方案。它们的目的是阻止懒惰的垃圾邮件发送者和低级攻击，但任何有足够兴趣和资源的人都可以解决任何验证码。随着“人工智能”的普及，这肯定会变得更便宜、更容易获得，但这并不意味着验证码本质上是无用的。它们是永恒的猫捉老鼠游戏的一部分。

与法学硕士一样，他们依赖于某些信号可能表明可疑行为的概率。像 Turnstile 这样复杂的解决方案会分析大量数据，可能还使用 LLM 来检测伪随机键盘输入，因此它们比您的定制解决方案更有效。它们并不完美，并且可能会出现误报，但不幸的是，这是每个人都必须为现代互联网上的合法用户可用的服务付出的代价。

我确实担心这些服务会获得大量敏感数据，这些数据可能会被滥用于跟踪用户、广告等，但您可以自行托管 OSS 替代品来缓解这种情况。

So your solution is to deploy a black box that can be worked around with a basic lookup table for a single field?

CAPTCHAs were never meant to work 100% of the time in all situations, or be the only security solution. They're meant to block lazy spammers and low-level attacks, but anyone with enough interest and resources can work around any CAPTCHA. This is certainly becoming cheaper and more accessible with the proliferation of "AI", but it doesn't mean that CAPTCHAs are inherently useless. They're part of a perpetual cat and mouse game.

Like LLMs, they rely on probabilities that certain signals may indicate suspicious behavior. Sophisticated ones like Turnstile analyze a lot of data, likely using LLMs to detect pseudorandom keyboard input as well, so they would be far more effective than your bespoke solution. They're not perfect, and can have false positives, but this is unfortunately the price everyone has to pay for services to be available to legitimate users on the modern internet.

I do share a concern that these services are given a lot of sensitive data which could potentially be abused for tracking users, advertising, etc., but there are OSS alternatives you can self-host that mitigate this.

chw9e 2026/04/02 14:50
这件事几年前就发生在我身上。一夜之间我就订阅了大约 700 份时事通讯。在所有注册过程中，我的爱彼迎帐户出现了活动，我的通知设置发生了更改。当我检查我的爱彼迎时，我注意到有人在我的帐户下创建了一个虚假列表，并禁用了它的预订通知。这是一个真正的多层骗局，黑客可以通过其他人帐户上的虚假列表赚钱，而其他人可能永远不会意识到这一点。
This happened to me several years ago. I got signed up to probably 700 newsletters overnight. In the middle of all of the sign ups there was activity on my airbnb account where my notification settings were changed. when i checked my airbnb i noticed that someone had created a fake listing under my account and disabled booking notifications for it. a real multi-layer scam where the hacker would be making money off a fake listing on someone else's account who would probably never even realize it.

Gigachad 2026/04/02 14:56
我可能不会受到这种情况的影响，因为我设置了电子邮件过滤器，因此任何带有取消订阅链接的内容都会被移至垃圾邮件。

帐户通知电子邮件不会取消订阅，而几乎所有垃圾邮件都会取消订阅。

I’d be probably safe against this because I have an email filter set so anything with an unsubscribe link gets moved to spam.

Account notification emails don’t have unsubscribes while pretty much all junk does.

noname120 2026/04/02 16:18

I receive a bunch of transactional emails with an unsubscribe button at the bottom. Unsubscribing of course does nothing to stop the transactional emails but the last thing I want is them to go to spams.

linolevan 2026/04/02 12:54
关于攻击向量的文章写得很好，我以前从未认真考虑过。感谢您详细说明 为什么 向随机的人发送一两封电子邮件可以帮助攻击者实现他们的目标。很多类似的文章都会跳过这样的细节。
Well written piece on an attack vector I'd never thought too hard about before. Thanks for elaborating on why sending an email or two to a random person helps an attacker achieve their goal. A lot of similar articles skip over details like that.

I had similar situation on WooCommerce shop. But it was much more signups per hour. Putting turnstile in front fixed problem.

My conclusion is to move from WordPress software as fast as possible, every WordPress site I manage gets bombarded by bots.

在 HN 中查看
avian 2026/04/02 15:49
> 目标是让受害者的收件箱充满太多噪音，以致他们无法找到真正重要的电子邮件。

> 当受害者溺水时 [...] 攻击者正在做其他事情。

在过去的几个月中，我管理的邮件服务器上的一些个人邮件帐户成为了与此处描述的内容类似的问题的受害者。

数百封邮件显然来自各种（看似合法的）随机公共网络服务、支持请求、问题跟踪器、网络联系表格等。例如，其中很大一部分来自弗吉尼亚州机动车辆管理局（例如“感谢您向我们提交文件#123”）。

更奇怪的是，它们并没有直接发送到该地址，而是根据邮件标题通过 Google 群组退回（每次我检查相关群组都已被删除）。据我所知，输入这些网站的不是我服务器上托管的邮件地址。

没有网络钓鱼链接，没有附加的恶意软件，没有潜入文本字段的短广告等。只有来自“noreply@”合法实体的大量自动回复。

我见过几次此类攻击，并花了一些时间对其进行调查。据我所知，正如文章作者提到的那样，这些与任何其他恶意活动无关。如果有的话，它们只是对邮箱的拒绝服务攻击（例如，让人类用户在垃圾中搜寻，邮件量远未使服务器本身饱和）。我不知道到底是什么动机，除了让小型邮件服务器管理员的生活变得比现在更加困难之外。

> The goal [...] to flood the victim’s inbox with so much noise that they can’t find the emails that actually matter.

> While the victim is drowning [...] the attacker is doing something else.

In the past months some personal mail accounts on a mail server I administer were victim of something that looked similar to what's described here.

Hundreds of mails apparently originating from various (legit-looking) random public web services, support requests, issue trackers, web contact forms etc. For example, a good part of them was from Virginia Department of Motor Vehicles (as in something like "thank you for filing a document #123 with us").

To make things even weirder, they were not sent directly to the address, but according to message headers were bounced through Google Groups (each time I checked the relevant group was already deleted). So as far as I can tell it was not the mail address hosted on my server that was being entered into those websites.

No phishing links, no attached malware, no short advertisements snuck into a text field etc. Just a huge amount of automated replies from "noreply@" legit entities.

I've seen several of these attacks and spent some time investigating them. To my knowledge these were not associated with any other malicious activity, like the author of the article mentions. If anything they were just a denial-of-service attack on a mail box (as in, making the human user trawl through garbage, the mail volume was far from saturating the server itself). What exactly would be a motivation for that I can't tell, except making the life of a small mail server admin even harder than it already is.

在 HN 中查看
mads_quist 2026/04/02 13:13
一个很好的旧蜜罐帮助我们在 All Quiet 应对这些攻击“很多”。基本上所有的攻击都可以通过这个来修复。不需要 Cloudflare 等。
A good old Honey Pot helped us at All Quiet "a lot" with those attacks. Basically all attacks are remediated by this. No need for Cloudflare etc.
在 HN 中查看
grey-area 2026/04/02 13:15
你能扩展一下吗？一个对真实用户不可见的单独的蜜罐注册页面，还是其他什么？
Can you expand on that? A separate honey pot sign up page invisible to real users, or something else?
在 HN 中查看
mads_quist 2026/04/02 13:17
您可以将“隐藏”输入添加到 HTML 表单中，这些输入的名称类似于“名字”或“姓氏”。机器人会填写它们。您要么期望它们为空，要么通过 JavaScript 填充您期望的内容。它当然是可逆向工程的，但确实有效。
You add "hidden" inputs to your HTML form that are named like "First Name" or "Family Name". Bots will fill them out. You will either expect them to be empty or you fill by JavaScript with sth you expect. It's of course reverse-engineerable, but does the trick.
在 HN 中查看
alexjurkiewicz 2026/04/02 13:30
这不会破坏密码管理器自动填充吗？
Doesn't that break password manager autofill?
在 HN 中查看
imhoguy 2026/04/02 16:02
请注意，它可能会破坏您的服务的可访问性。如果有人填写这些字段，我会添加额外的验证，例如易于访问的验证码。
Watch out, it may break accessibility of your service. If somebody fills these fields I would add extra verification e.g. accessible CAPTCHA.
在 HN 中查看
grey-area 2026/04/02 13:26
谢谢，不幸的是，我已经看到脚本化攻击绕过了这种隐藏输入（可能是人工辅助，或者可能只是忽略了隐藏字段）。
Thanks, I’ve seen scripted attacks bypass this sort of hidden input unfortunately (perhaps human assisted or perhaps just ignoring hidden fields).
在 HN 中查看
jaggederest 2026/04/02 14:39
它们实际上通常会忽略真正隐藏的字段（输入类型=隐藏），但如果您将它们放在带有 css 的元素“后面”，或者非常小但仍然呈现，许多人都会被捕获。这类似于人们对法学硕士所做的厚颜无耻的即时注入攻击。
They often do actually ignore truly hidden fields (input type=hidden) but if you put them "behind" an element with css, or extremely small but still rendered, many get caught. It's similar to the cheeky prompt injection attacks people did/do against LLMs.
在 HN 中查看
grey-area 2026/04/02 15:45
谢谢。
Thanks.

mads_quist 2026/04/02 13:32
当然，这当然是非常基本的。
Sure, it's really basic of course.

Do you test this against password managers? Seems like this approach could generate false positives
在 HN 中查看
hrmtst93837 2026/04/02 16:40
蜜罐会一直工作，直到机器人开始向每个字段发帖为止。放弃流量清理还意味着您会失去 Cloudflare 等服务为您提供的滥用报告和 IP 声誉源，因此过滤一类注册垃圾邮件的技巧会变成您自己处理其余的混乱情况。
Honeypots work until the bot starts posting to every field. Dropping traffic scrubbing also means you lose the abuse reporting and IP reputation feed that a service like Cloudflare gives you, so a trick that filters one class of signup spam turns into you handling the rest of the mess yourself.

tantalor 2026/04/02 23:21
减轻这种情况是毫无意义的，因为很多网站都容易受到这种情况的影响。

不久前，我收到了大约十几个 .gov 网站的订阅轰炸。 Healthcare.gov、社会保障管理局、WTC 健康计划、FDA、国家心理健康研究所、Medicare.gov。

攻击者很容易做到这一点。努力解决一个站点的这一问题并期望产生任何影响，就像试图用茶匙倒空海洋一样。

Mitigating this is kind of pointless, because so many sites are vulnerable to this.

I got subscription bombed from about a dozen .gov sites a while back. Healthcare.gov, Social Security Administration, WTC Health Program, FDA, National Institute of Mental Health, Medicare.gov.

It's very easy for attackers to do this. Putting in effort to solve this for one site, and expecting any impact, is like trying to empty the ocean with a teaspoon.

alsetmusic 2026/04/03 00:21

You mitigate because you want to be responsible and not contribute to a problem. One person has to be the first to do something better before it can become a thing that is a best practice throughout an entire industry.

veyh 2026/04/02 21:31
哦，这实际上解释了很多。我曾经为我的一个应用程序提供 7 天免费试用，您可以通过电子邮件注册，但绝大多数许可证从未使用过。该应用程序甚至没有被下载多次。

我停止提供试用版，因为我认为它对于相对便宜（19 欧元）且具有 30 天退款保证的应用程序没有用处。 （无需许可证也可以使用15分钟，但之后需要重新启动。）

Oh, that actually explains a lot. I used to offer a 7-day free trial for one of my apps that you could sign up for with an email, and the vast majority of the licenses were never used. The app wasn't even downloaded that many times.

I stopped offering the trial since I figured it was not useful for a relatively cheap (19 EUR) app that has a 30-day money-back guarantee anyway. (It can also be used for 15 mins without a license, but requires a restart after that.)

rglullis 2026/04/02 20:59
这就解释了我在 Communick 上随机注册的情况。该模式也完全符合我所看到的情况（一小时内有 3-4 个注册，奇怪的用户名和 gmail/hotmail 地址，其中有很多通常被忽略的“。”。至少就我而言，缓解措施来自于我对 不是 收集任何不必要的数据。电子邮件地址是可选的，仅当您已经是付费订阅者时才使用。

也许我应该将其从注册表单中完全删除并将其用作蜜罐。

So that explains the handful of random sign-ups I am getting on Communick. The pattern fits exactly what I am seeing as well (3-4 signups in an hour, weird usernames and gmail/hotmail addresses with lots of "." that are usually ignored. At least on my case the mitigation comes with my obsession in not collecting any unnecessary data. Email address are optional and only used if you are already a paying subscriber.

Maybe I should just remove it from the sign-up form altogether and use it as a honeypot.

dgellow 2026/04/02 18:49
哦，这很有趣。我认为这与我运行的微型 saas 的体验完美匹配。我首先认为人们是有机地发现它的，因为它开始时整个周末只有几个注册，然后最终升级到每小时多个。没有一个帐户处于活跃状态，但电子邮件地址看起来并不太奇怪，而且也没有退回。我最终添加了一个似乎有效的验证码，但这是一次令人惊讶的体验，因为据我所知，您无法将该 saas 用于任何邪恶的事情
Oh that’s interesting. I think that matches perfectly with an experience I had with a micro saas I run. I first thought people discovered it organically because it started with just a few signups throughout the weekend, then eventually escalated to multiple an hour. None of the accounts were active but email addresses didn’t look too odd and they weren’t bouncing. I eventually added a captcha that seems to have been effective, but that was a surprising experience because there is nothing you can use that saas for anything nefarious as far as I’m aware
在 HN 中查看
tmarice 2026/04/02 21:06
我不太理解验证码的讨厌之处，它是任何面向公众的表单的赌注。您需要在“注册简便性”与“安全性”曲线上选择一个点，而电子邮件注册+验证码似乎是最佳选择。

我还没有看到任何证据表明大型企业（Google、CF）可以轻松自动绕过，并且如果有人有相反的证据，我很乐意了解更多信息。

I don't really understand the captcha hate, it's table stakes for any public-facing form. You need to pick a point on the "Ease of signup" vs. "Security" curve, and email signup + captcha seems to be the sweet spot.

I haven't seen any proof that the big ones (Google, CF) can be easily and automatically bypassed, and would love to learn more if someone has evidence to the contrary.

withinboredom 2026/04/02 21:19
我的博客在您的浏览器中提交（withinboredom.info）之前会进行工作证明。它会使用相当多的 CPU 功率，但只需要几秒钟即可完成。对于攻击者来说……这是相当缓慢且自我限制的。
My blog does a proof-of-work before submission (withinboredom.info) in your browser. It'll use a fair bit of cpu power, but should only take a few seconds to complete. For an attacker... that's quite slow and self-limiting.

So the user doesn’t receives anything.

在 HN 中查看
aservus 2026/04/02 16:21
具有讽刺意味的是，最容易受到此影响的服务是那些首先收集最多电子邮件/数据的服务。最少的数据收集是最好的缓解措施。
The irony is that the services most vulnerable to this are the ones that collect the most email/data in the first place. Minimal data collection is the best mitigation.

halapro 2026/04/02 22:26

在 HN 中查看
msephton 2026/04/02 13:47
受影响的用户如何从此类攻击中恢复？
How can an affected user recover from such an attack?
在 HN 中查看
jeroenhd 2026/04/02 14:52
将所有收到的电子邮件报告为垃圾邮件，添加过滤器以删除@domain.com 电子邮件，并可能将整个服务添加到黑名单中，这样就不会再发生这种情况。可能会删除该服务上可能存在的任何付费帐户。

如果电子邮件的目标域使用来自任何主要电子邮件提供商（Gmail、Outlook）的邮件服务器，则用户可能会发现大多数电子邮件无论如何都会进入垃圾邮件文件夹，并且发送域将自动添加到垃圾邮件列表中。特别是当攻击攻击同一电子邮件服务上的多个用户时。

Report all received emails as spam, add a filter to get rid of the @domain.com emails, and probably add the entire service to the blacklist so it doesn't happen again. Probably get rid of any paid account on that service one might have laying around.

If the email is targeted at a domain using mail servers from any major email provider (Gmail, Outlook), the user will probably find most emails into their spam folder anyway and the sending domain will get added to the spam list automatically. Especially if the attack hits multiple users on the same email service.

cuu508 2026/04/02 12:59
> 如果机器人使用其他人的电子邮件创建帐户，受害者会收到一封电子邮件，如果他们忽略它，那就结束了。欢迎电子邮件及其后的所有内容仅在用户验证后才会触发。

作为用户，我根本不希望收到欢迎电子邮件。

> If a bot creates an account with someone else’s email, the victim gets one email, if they ignore it that’s the end of it. The welcome email and everything after it only fires once the user verifies.

As a user, I would prefer no welcome email at all.

在 HN 中查看
SebastianKra 2026/04/02 14:55
我怀疑除了 SaaS 提供商之外，每个人都有这种感觉。他们可能只是给你一个复选框来关闭时事通讯，但他们没有。
I suspect everyone feels that way except SaaS providers. They could just give you a checkbox to turn the newsletter off, but they don't.
在 HN 中查看
tomjen3 2026/04/02 14:06
是的，这就是我讨厌“使用服务登录”的部分原因。最大的好处是不会向我发送垃圾邮件，但他们总是坚持收到我的电子邮件。

曾经有一段时间，您必须选择“为我注册您的时事通讯”，然后您必须取消选中它。然后你必须检查是否收到电子邮件，而现在你甚至没有这个选择。

最近呢？你必须仔细检查你的电子邮件，因为你无法设置密码（看着你，克劳德），所以你无法过滤电子邮件。

Yeah, thats part of why I hate "login with SERVICE". The big benefit would be not spamming me, but they always insist on getting my email.

There was a time were you would have to select "sign me up for your newsletter" then you had to uncheck it. Then you had to check to not get an email and now you don't even get that choice.

And lately? You have to go dig through your email because you can't set a password (looking at you Claude), so you can't filter email.

sodapopcan 2026/04/02 13:40
是的，正确。 当我点击链接时，我已经受到欢迎页面的欢迎（在很大程度上，这是欢迎的）。 但那为什么还要再给我发一封电子邮件来进一步欢迎我呢？ 我已经感到受欢迎了！ 不要给我任何“因为它有效”的废话（尽管这就是你要说的）。

（cuu508，这里的“你”不是指你）

Yes, correct. When I clicked the link I was already welcomed by the welcome page (which is, for the most part, welcomed). But then why send me another email further welcoming me? I already feel welcomed! And don't give me any of that "because it works" BS (even though that is what you are going to say).

(cuu508, "you" in this instance does not mean you)

在 HN 中查看
pagecalm 2026/04/02 23:45
老实说，在地址得到确认之前，不要发送任何超过验证电子邮件的内容。这里的许多其他修复正在解决实际上只是信任注册地址的身份验证库的问题。感觉这是值得首先解决的问题。
Honestly just don't send anything past the verification email until the address is confirmed. A lot of the other fixes here are working around what's really just auth libraries trusting the address on signup. Feels like that's the thing worth fixing first.
在 HN 中查看
thisisnow 2026/04/02 17:01
有趣的是，在它变成 Cloudflare 的广告之前，它像瘟疫一样在互联网上传播，速度每天都变慢，迫使 JS 并试图从浏览器中提取每个数据点。这真的是唯一的解决方案吗？
Interesting, until it turned into an ad for Cloudflare, it spreads like plague on the internet, slowing everyday down, forcing JS and trying to pull every single datapoint from your browser. Is this really the _only_ solution?
在 HN 中查看
queenkjuul 2026/04/02 13:14
我的电子邮件在这样的攻击中被盗，我仍然随机收到“你放弃了你的购物车！”时不时地发电子邮件，但幸运的是（？）他们同时收到了我的信用卡，我在几分钟内取消了它。所以这有点烦人，但对我来说，洪水起作用并没有真正的意义。至少不是使用美国信用卡，这些信用卡经常标记我自己的微型中心之旅，哈哈

编辑补充：这些电子邮件几乎 100% 来自同一个电子商务产品，我得查一下是哪一个。但我收到电子邮件的每个网站都运行相同的现成模板。

I had my email stolen in such an attack, i still get random "you abandoned your cart!" Emails now and then, but luckily (?) they got my credit card at the same time and i cancelled it within minutes. So it's a little annoyance, but it doesn't really make sense to me that the flood works. At least not with American credit cards that are routinely flagging my own trips to microcenter lol

Editing to add: almost 100% of these emails came from the same e-commerce product, I'll have to look up which. But every site i got an email from was running the same off the shelf template.

在 HN 中查看
CrzyLngPwd 2026/04/02 15:21
我们也经历过类似的事情；短时间内创建了数千个新账户，但正是 Amazon SES 向我们发送了有关投诉数量的警告，才让我们意识到这一点。

我们添加了验证码并使用一次性电子邮件检查服务来摆脱它。

We experienced a similar thing; Thousands of new accounts were being created over a short period, but it was Amazon SES sending us a warning about complaint numbers that woke us up to it.

We added a captcha and used a disposable email checking service to get rid of it.

radku 2026/04/02 15:31
同意。就我个人而言，我认为用 LLM 处理文本就像对图片应用滤镜一样。

Agreed. Personally I think about it as massaging a text with LLM is like applying filters to your pictures.

The text probably have been based entirely on the internal notes and investigations and is very informative. Would it be better if the OP wrote it entirely by themselves? Not necessarily.

denismi 2026/04/02 13:47
我非常有信心以下不是LLM：

> 新用户注册了，但没有做任何事情，他们没有创建组织、项目或部署，他们只是留下了一个帐户。

当然LLM版本是：

> 新用户注册了但没有做任何事情；他们并没有创建一个组织、一个项目或一个部署——他们只是留下了一个帐户。

I am quite confident that the following was NOT LLM:

> New users were signing up but not doing anything, they weren’t creating an org, a project, or a deployment, they just left an account sitting there.

Surely the LLM version is:

> New users were signing up but not doing anything; they weren't creating an org, a project, or a deployment—they just left an account sitting there.
